title: Joint Unitarity and a Single Definite Outcome in a Quantum Measurement
abstract: We investigated the possibility that a single measurement run with a definite outcome is a joint unitary evolution of all the participating systems, and measurement runs with different definite outcomes correspond to different unitary maps. With reasonable assumptions, we derived a lower bound of the dependence of the environment after measurement on the state of the system before measurement, conditioned on the same measurement outcome. An experimental test of this dependence relation can either serve as evidence that the unitary dynamics and the definite outcome in the orthodox sense cannot be true together or suggest a deviation from the von Neumann measurement model plus a "conditioning" interpretational step.
summary: This paper investigates whether a single quantum measurement run with a definite outcome can be described by a joint unitary evolution. It establishes that if such a unitary process exists, the final state of the environment must retain a trace of the initial system state. This provides a way to experimentally test the compatibility of unitary dynamics with the emergence of definite outcomes.
main_problem: Resolving the discrepancy between the von Neumann measurement model, which predicts entangled states, and the empirical reality of single, definite measurement outcomes.
main_result: The author derives a lower bound on the dependence of the post-measurement environment state on the initial system state, providing an experimentally testable signature of unitary dynamics in individual measurement runs.
method: The study uses mathematical tools such as the trace norm, diamond norm, and the gentle measurement lemma to analyze information transfer and derive bounds on state distinguishability.
model_or_system: A tripartite system consisting of a measured system (S), a measurement apparatus (A), and an environment (E), where different measurement outcomes are driven by different underlying unitary maps.
key_observables: The trace distance between the reduced final states of the environment conditioned on the same measurement outcome.
important_parameters: delta (state deviation), eta (environment fluctuation), and gamma (unitary mechanism fluctuation).
main_assumptions: The multiplicity assumption (different outcomes correspond to different unitaries) and the universality requirement (the mechanism for an outcome is independent of the input state).
paper_structure: The paper identifies the problem of outcomes, proposes an alternative unitary model with outcome-dependent maps, derives a mathematical lower bound for environment state dependence, and discusses experimental observability and noise limitations.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, experimentally testable criterion to distinguish between the standard von Neumann/conditioning interpretation and models where individual measurement runs are fundamentally unitary.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
